chunk_summary: Adolescents with food allergies face adherence risks and ARFID screening challenges, requiring tailored management strategies to address autonomy, risk perception, and nutritional compromise.
learning_objectives: Understand adolescent-specific adherence risks in food allergy management, including social and psychological factors.
Identify warning signs of avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID) in adolescents on elimination diets.
Develop safety plans for adolescents, including allergen recognition, anaphylaxis response, and structured school planning.
key_facts: Adolescents are at high risk for accidental exposure due to inconsistent avoidance and stigma-related behaviors.
ARFID screening should include signs like reduced food variety, weight loss, prolonged meal times, and eating-related distress.
Escalation to more restrictive diets is unsafe if ARFID warning signs are present; instead, simplify the diet and involve multidisciplinary support.
Teens must recognize early anaphylaxis symptoms, including cough or wheeze post-exposure, especially with comorbid asthma.
Structured school planning and periodic re-training reduce preventable allergic events.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Adolescents are a high-risk group for both accidental exposure and nutritional compromise because autonomy increases faster than risk perception. (Source: Psychosocial and quality-of-life impact of pediatric food allergy — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5867261/)
document_key_topics: Adolescent adherence risks in food allergy management
Safety planning for food allergy in adolescents
